segments.s092-g25: بگفتند is a simple past-reporting verb 'they said' introducing a reported clause. کاین is an archaic fused form of 'که این' (that this/that), introducing the content of what was said. تیر is the noun 'arrow' and زالست is the name Zal plus the copula -ست (is), so 'that this arrow is Zal's / is Zal'. The short clause ends with و بس, an idiomatic tag meaning 'and that's enough' or 'that is sufficient', functioning as a closing remark.
segments.s092-g26: نراند is the past form of راند with the negative prefix 'ن-' (here meaning 'did not drive' / 'was not driven'); the verb precedes its indefinite subject (کس) at the end, a common Classical Persian word order for emphasis. چنین ('such/so') modifies تیر ('arrow'); در کمان ('into/inside the bow') is a prepositional phrase showing where the arrow would be placed. The clause reads literally 'did not drive such an arrow into the bow (any)one', i.e. 'no one ever put such an arrow in a bow.'
segments.s092-g27: چو here means 'when' (temporal conjunction). خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective ('the shining sun'). ز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from above/on high'. بگشت is a past verb meaning 'turned' / 'came out/appeared' / 'rose' in this context; the exact lexical sense given in the supplied list did not match, so it functions here as the past action 'appeared/returned/rose' of the sun.
segments.s092-g28: خروش is the noun 'roar/cry/tumult' and stands as the subject of برآمد ('arose/issued forth'), a simple past. تبیره is an attributive word modifying خروش (its precise lexicographic sense isn’t represented in the provided senses), so I mark its sense as unknown here; ز دشت is the source phrase 'from the plain'. The clause means 'a (descriptive) cry arose from the plain.'
segments.s092-g29: به + شهر اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/at the inner city' (به = to/into; شهر اندرون = inner part of the city). کوس ('war-drum') is the instrument named, and با کرنای ('with trumpets') adds accompaniment: با = 'with' and کرنای (the oblique/construct form of کرنا) = 'trumpet(s)'. The whole segment is a fronted locative + instrument phrase that will be realized by the following verb ('sounded').
segments.s092-g30: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/verb meaning 'to roar/sound' used as the main action here ('sounded/roared out'). زنگ ('bells') and هندی ('Indian' — adjectival, here qualifying instruments/styles) are coordinated by و. درای in the supplied senses list does not match the expected instrument-word here, so I mark its sense unknown; syntactically this phrase lists the instruments that 'cried out' or 'made a clamor.'
segments.s092-g31: برآمد is the past 'arose/appeared' or 'advanced' here used with سپه را (the army as definite object marked by را). The phrase به هامون کشید is a motion clause: به هامون = 'onto/to the plain' and کشید (past of کشیدن) = 'drew/moved' — together indicating they moved/advanced the army onto the plain. Word order places the object (سپه را) before the destination phrase and verb, a classical arrangement.
segments.s092-g32: This coordinate noun phrase 'سراپرده و پیل' lists the things acted on: سراپرده ('inner tent/curtain') and پیل ('elephant(s)'). بیرون کشید is the verbal phrase 'pulled out/brought out' (بیرون = out, کشید = pulled/brought), so the clause means 'they brought forth the inner tent (or opened the curtain) and the elephants.'
segments.s092-g33: سپاه اندرآورد is a past, third‑person action: '[he] brought (forth) an army/host.' The following prekpositional phrase پیش سپاه (= 'in front of the army') marks the position/target of that deployment. Word order is slightly poetic but equals 'he brought an army before the army.'
segments.s092-g34: چو here is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. هامون شد literally 'the Hamun became' (i.e. appeared/was seen), and از گرد کوه سیاه is a prepositional phrase 'from around the black mountain' specifying where Hamun was seen. Note the normal Persian order: conjunction + predicate + source/locative phrase.
segments.s092-g35: This is a nominal phrase naming the fighters: خزروان دمان is a compound identifier ('the Khazruvans of Daman' / a group/name), followed by an instrumental phrase با عمود و سپر 'with spear and shield.' با marks the instruments; عمود and سپر are the items joined by و.
segments.s092-g36: یکی is 'one (of them),' acting as the subject. تاختن کرد is a periphrastic past (to make a charge / he charged) with کرد as the auxiliary. بر introduces the adversative object 'against' and زال زر names the addressee ('Zāl, the Golden').
segments.s092-g37: عمودی is used as a substantive/adjective 'the upright (spear)' and بزد is the simple past 'he struck.' The doubled بر بر is a poetic repetition of the preposition 'on/upon,' and روشنش is روشن + ـش (his) meaning 'his shining/bright (part)' — here the target of the blow.
segments.s092-g38: گسسته شد is a stative/result clause 'was broken / split apart' (گسسته 'broken' + شد auxiliary). آن نامور جوشنش is the subject in inverted order: 'that famed armor of his' (جوشن + ـش = his cuirass). The line thus reports the armor's breaking as the result of the strike.
segments.s092-g39: چو شد introduces a temporal clause 'when ... became / shone.' تافته is a predicative adjective 'radiant / shining' placed before شاه ('king'), so the clause reads 'when the king of Zabulistan became radiant / was revealed.' This is a common poetic inversion emphasizing the adjective.
segments.s092-g40: برفتند is the past, third‑person plural 'they went / advanced.' It is followed by the noun phrase گردان کابلستان 'the contingents/troops of Kabulistan,' which is the subject/agent; the verb–subject order here is a typical poetic inversion meaning 'the Kabulistan regiments advanced.'
segments.s092-g41: "یکی" here is an indefinite introducer meaning 'someone/a certain one' placed before the action; "درع پوشید" is a simple past verb phrase (پوشید = he put on/ wore) with the object درع (armor) appearing before the verb for poetic emphasis. The subject "زال دلیر" (Zal the brave) follows the verb because Persian poetry often allows verb/object/subject rearrangement; دلیر is an epithet attached to the name.
segments.s092-g42: "به جنگ اندر آمد" is a locative+verb sequence meaning 'entered into battle' (اندر adds a poetic 'into/within'); به takes the 'towards/into' sense. "به کردار شیر" is a simile 'in the manner/conduct of a lion' where به + کردار makes an adverbial phrase and کردار ('behavior/conduct') is qualified by شیر ('lion'). آمد is past tense 'he came/entered.'
segments.s092-g43: The prepositional phrase "به دست اندرون" ('in/at the inner hand') indicates location/instrument and precedes the verb داشت (past of داشتن, 'he had/held'). "گرز پدر" is a possessive construction 'the father's mace' (گرز = mace, پدر = father) with the possessor following the possessed, common in poetic diction.
segments.s092-g44: "سرش گشته" means 'his head had become' where ش is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to سر and گشته is the perfect/participial form of گشتن ('become'). The pattern "پر X" means 'full of X' (پر خشم = full of anger; پر خون جگر = full of blood in the jigar/heart). جگر here is used figuratively as 'heart/seat of feeling.'
segments.s092-g45: بزد is the 3rd-person past 'he struck.' The prepositional phrase "بر سرش" ('on his head') precedes the object "گرزه گاورنگ" ('tawny mace'), where گاورنگ is a color epithet modifying گرزه. ش is the enclitic 'his' on سر. Word order (prepositional phrase + object + verb) is shaped by poetic rhythm.
segments.s092-g46: زمین شد is 'the ground became' (شد = became). "ز خونش" is a causative/from-phrase 'from his blood' (ز = from; خونش = his blood). چو is the comparative 'like/as,' and "پشت پلنگ" ('the back of a leopard') provides the simile; overall the clause is inverted for poetic effect (literally: 'the ground became from his blood like the back of a leopard').
segments.s092-g47: بیفگند and بسپرد are successive 3rd-person past verbs ('he threw/cast' and 'he entrusted/handed over'), linked by و. The short sequence زو درگذشت is archaic phrasing: ز + او (here split as ز / و in the tokenization) means 'from him' or 'away from him,' and درگذشت is the past 'he passed away/died.' The verbs are telic past actions presented in quick succession (typical heroic narrative compression).
segments.s092-g48: "ز پیش سپاه" means 'from before the army/host' (ز = from, پیش = before/in front of, سپاه = army) and functions as a locative/ablative phrase. "اندر آمد به دشت" is 'entered into the plain' (اندر آمد = entered/ came in; به دشت = into the plain). The clause order is typical poetic placement of prepositional phrases before the verb.
segments.s092-g9: پس آنگه سوی شهر بنهاد روی is a compact clause: پس آنگه (then) provides a connective temporal adverb, سوی شهر marks direction ('toward the city'), and بنهاد روی is a verbal phrase literally 'placed/put (his) face' — an older poetic way to say 'turned his face toward'. بنهاد is a past-tense verb meaning 'set/placed', and روی is the noun 'face' following it.
segments.s092-g10: چو آمد به شهر اندرون نامجوی is ordered in an older Persian V (verb)–S (subject) pattern: چو آمد ('when/when he came') is the temporal clause, به شهر اندرون means 'into the inner part of the city', and نامجوی is a proper name following the verb as subject (common in epic style) — 'Namjuy came into the inner city.'
segments.s092-g11: به مهراب گفت is 'he said to Mehrab': به marks the indirect object (to/for), مهراب here is a proper name (addressed person), and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. ای is the vocative particle 'O', and هشیوار مرد is the vocative noun phrase 'alert/wise man' addressing Mehrab.
segments.s092-g12: پسندیده اندر همه کارکرد is a descriptive phrase modifying the addressee: پسندیده 'approved/esteemed' is an adjective or participial adjective, اندر همه 'in all' is a prepositional phrase ('in all [things]'), and کارکرد is 'deeds/actions' — together 'esteemed/approved in all his actions.'
segments.s092-g13: کنون من شوم در شب تیره‌گون: کنون 'now' fronted for emphasis, من is the first‑person pronoun, and شوم is a present/subjunctive or optative form of شدن ('may I become' / 'I will be'), used here as a volitional/future sense. در شب تیره‌گون is a locative phrase 'in the dark/gloomy night' modifying شوم. تیره‌گون is an adjectival compound ('dark‑hued / gloomy') typical of poetic diction.
segments.s092-g14: یکی دست یازم بریشان به خون: یکی 'someone/a certain one' is indefinite, دست یازم is a compound verb meaning 'I will lay hold (lit. 'reach hand')' where یازم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative form used for intent in poetry. بریشان is a contracted older form (بر + ایشان) meaning 'upon/against them', and به خون 'with blood' indicates causing bloodshed. The clause expresses intent to seize someone and cause bloodshed.
segments.s092-g15: شوند آگه از من که بازآمدم: شوند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'let them become / may they be', forming a hortative/wish; آگه 'aware' complements شوند (i.e., 'let them become aware'), از من 'of me', and که بازآمدم introduces the content clause 'that I returned' where بازآمدم is a perfect/past 'I have returned / I returned.'
segments.s092-g16: دل آگنده و کینه ساز آمدم is the speaker's self‑description linked to the previous clause: دل آگنده 'with a heart filled' (an adjective phrase), و connects, and کینه ساز is a compound meaning 'maker/creator of hatred' or 'vengeful' (ساز here is an agentive/device sense). آمدم is the past tense 'I came' — together 'I came with a full heart and bent on hatred.'
segments.s092-g57: بزد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb ‘he struck/ smote’ and is the main action of the line. بر is the preposition ‘on/at’; کمربند کلباد is a noun phrase ‘Kalbad’s belt’ (کمربند = belt, followed by the name کلباد). The final بر repeats the preposition and links this action forward to the next phrase (the same verb بزد governs both segments).
segments.s092-g58: بران is an adverbial/locative ‘thereupon/on that’; بند here means ‘link/fetter’ (a unit of a chain). زنجیر پولاد = ‘steel chain’ (زنجیر = chain, پولاد = steel). The final بر again is the locative preposition ‘on/at’. The verb بزد from the previous segment is understood to apply to this phrase as well (omitted repetition).
segments.s092-g59: میانش is a locative expression ‘in its middle’ or ‘between them’ (میان + ـش enclitic). ابا کوهه appears as a compound noun or descriptive phrase placed after میانش (classical word order), and زین بدوخت is a verb phrase ‘he sewed the saddle’ (زین = saddle, بدوخت = sewed). The verb بدوخت is simple past; the whole segment says that something (a pad/cover) was sewn into the middle of the saddle/thing.
segments.s092-g60: سپه را marks ‘the army’ as a definite object (را). به کلباد is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/for Kalbad’ and بر دل is another locative phrase ‘upon the heart’ (دل = heart). بسوخت is past tense ‘was burned’/‘burned’ used intransitively in the idiom ‘دل سوختن’ meaning ‘the heart was inflamed/pained’; here it says the army’s heart was inflamed (with feeling) toward Kalbad. Word order places the object with را before the adverbial phrases, and the verb closes the clause.
segments.s092-g61: چو is a subordinating conjunction ‘when’. این دو = ‘these two’ is the subject. سرافگنده شد is a compound of adjective + شد: سرافگنده ‘ashamed/humiliated’ + شد ‘became’ (past), so ‘they became ashamed’. در نبرد is a locative phrase ‘in battle’. The clause is temporal: ‘When these two became ashamed in battle…’.
segments.s092-g62: شماساس is the proper noun/agent (here the named man). شد is the past-tense copula ‘became’. بی‌دل is a predicative adjective ‘without heart / heartbroken’ (here meaning distraught or despondent). و joins the next predicate; روی زرد is a noun + adjective ‘face pale’ (روی = face, زرد = pale/wan). The two predicates describe his changed emotional/physical state.
segments.s092-g63: This is a coordinated noun phrase: شماساس و آن لشکر — ‘Shamasas and that army’. رزم ساز is a compound modifier after لشکر: رزم (battle) + ساز (maker/device) forming an adjectival phrase ‘battle-making’ or ‘war-ready’ describing the army. Classical word order places modifiers after the head noun in such compounds.
segments.s092-g64: پراگنده is an adjective ‘scattered’. از رزم is a prepositional phrase ‘from the battle’. گشتند is the past plural of گشتن; here it means ‘they turned/returned’ (they changed direction/state) and agrees with the plural subject. باز after the verb means ‘back/again’ (here ‘turned back’ or ‘retreated’).
segments.s092-g1: Subject + adverbial phrase + verb: فرستاده (the messenger) is the subject, followed by نزدیک دستان which functions as an adverbial phrase meaning 'near/close at hand', and رسید is the simple past (3rd person singular) 'arrived/reached'. Word order is typical Persian: subject then locative phrase then verb.
segments.s092-g2: This clause gives the mental/affective result: به here marks the agent/cause ('by/with' or 'to'), کردار آتش is a genitive-style grouping 'the fire (آتش) of action/deed (کردار)' meaning an ardent impulse to act, دلش 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش), and بردمید is a past verb meaning 'seized/took away' (here passive-in-meaning: his heart was taken/seized by that fire).
segments.s092-g3: سوی ... بنهاد روی is a verbal phrase 'he set his face toward ...': سوی marks direction ('toward'), گرد here functions as part of the directional phrase ('about/near' the precinct of), مهراب is the place (mihrab/sanctuary), بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put' (here 'set'), and روی is the object 'face'. The whole is idiomatic: 'he set his face toward the mihrab.'
segments.s092-g4: همی is an archaic/poetic adverb marking continued or immediate action (often glossed 'forthwith' or continuous 'was/kept doing'); تاخت is the past tense 'galloped/rushed/charged'; با + لشکری جنگجوی is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with a warlike army/host' (لشکری 'troops/host', جنگجوی 'warlike/warrior').
segments.s092-g5: چو = 'when'; مهراب را uses the object marker را to mark Mehrab as the entity seen; پای بر جای is an idiomatic unit 'foot upon the place' meaning 'standing in place' or 'planted firmly'; دید is the past 'he saw'. The clause literally: 'When he saw Mehrab's foot on the place...' i.e. 'when he saw Mehrab standing in his place.'
segments.s092-g6: به سرش اندرون = 'within his head' / 'in his mind' (به + سر + enclitic ش + اندرون = locative phrase). دانش و رای is a coordinated noun phrase 'knowledge and counsel/plan'; دید again is the verb 'he saw/perceived.' The construction reports that he perceived knowledge and counsel inside Mehrab's mind.
segments.s092-g7: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said to his heart' = 'he thought to himself / he told his heart'; کاکنون = 'now' (poetic ka+kanun), ز لشکر = 'of/from the army' (ز = 'from/of'), چه باک = rhetorical 'what fear' (چه as exclamatory 'what/how', باک = 'fear'). Note: گفت here records inner speech; the provided noun senses for گفت do not include the verbal 'said', so sense index is marked -1.
segments.s092-g8: This is a string of rhetorical questions. چه پیشم خزروان = 'what (are) kings before me?' (پیشم = 'before me / in my presence'); چه یک مشت خاک = 'what (are they) — a single handful of dust' (مشت خاک 'a handful of dust' = contemptuous minimization). Persian often omits the copula (are), so these lines are elliptical exclamations.
segments.s092-g49: شماساس را خواست is a transitive past clause: خواست (wanted/asked) takes شماساس as its definite object, marked by را. The following کاید برون is a noun phrase 'the plot/cunning (کاید) out/forth (برون)' functioning as the thing demanded — a compressed, poetic way to say 'he wanted the plot brought out'. The lack of a subordinating particle (مثلِ که) is typical of older/epic Persian.
segments.s092-g50: نیامد برون is a simple past negative: 'did not come out' (نیامد + adverb برون). برون کش is an imperative/infinitival verbal phrase 'pull/draw out' (کش = 'pull'); in context it contrasts with نیامد (he did not come out), then (someone) draws out a weapon. بخوشید is an archaic perfective/verbal adjective meaning 'took delight/was glad' and governs the following noun خون 'blood', i.e. 'rejoiced in blood.'
segments.s092-g51: به گرد اندرون is a locative/positional phrase: به 'into/towards' + گرد 'around/area' + اندرون 'inside/inner quarters' giving 'into the inner part'. یافت is the past of یافتن 'to find' and takes کلباد را as its definite object (را marks Kalbad as definite). Word order places the location phrase before the verb, as common in poetry.
segments.s092-g52: به گردن is another locative phrase 'on/around the neck' (به + گردن). برآورد here is a past transitive verb 'placed/put (on)' (in older Persian برآوردن can mean to set/raise/put upon) and takes پولاد را as its definite object (را marks the steel/metal object). The verb therefore applies to putting the steel item around the neck.
segments.s092-g53: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. شمشیرزن is the subject 'the swordsman'; گرز دستان is a compact descriptive object/phrase 'the mace (گرز) in (his) hands (دستان)' — the genitive/possessive relation is implied by word order and context (poetic compression). بدید is the simple past 'saw'.
segments.s092-g54: همی is a continuous/progressive marker often used in epic Persian (here giving an on‑going or immediate-action sense). کرد is the past of کردن; combined with همی it conveys 'did/made' (here 'caused'). ازو 'from/away from him' indicates the experiencer or reference point, خویشتن is the reflexive 'himself/one's self', and ناپدید is an adjectival/predicative complement 'vanished/invisible'. Together: 'he made himself vanish from him.'
segments.s092-g55: کمان را is the definite object 'the bow' (را marks it). به زه کرد is a light verb construction: به + زه (bowstring) + کرد ('did/made') = 'he strung (the bow)'. The subject زال سوار (Zal, the rider) follows the verb, a common poetic word order that places the epithet after the action.
segments.s092-g56: خدنگی is the instrument 'a dagger'; راند is the past 'drove/forced' and takes خدنگی as its object (instrumental action). بدو 'to him' is the indirect object/directional pronoun 'at him', and اندرون 'inward/into' modifies the motion (he thrust inward). خوار functions as a resultative/adjectival comment 'low/despised' — 'bringing him low' — and is placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s092-g81: ازان is the older/poetic form of از آن meaning 'from that' and here introduces the source ('from that army'). لشکر is the head noun 'host/army'; the adjectives خسته و بسته follow it and describe the army ('weary and beset/encircled'). مرد is the noun 'a man' (here introduced after the described army: 'from that army, a man...'). Word order is poetic but follows Persian logic: prepositional source (ازان + noun + adjectives) followed by the individual (مرد).
segments.s092-g82: به is the directional preposition 'toward/to' and خورشید تابان is the noun + postpositive adjective 'the shining sun' (تابان = 'shining'). برآورد is a 3rd person past (poetic) of برآوردن used here in the sense 'raised/turned (one's face)' rather than the literal senses listed; گرد is an archaic/poetic element indicating turning/going (so together برآورد گرد ≈ 'turned (his face)'). The verb is in past 3rd singular, matching a singular subject ('مرد').
segments.s092-g83: گریزان is an adjectival/participial form 'fleeing' placed before the name شماساس to modify it ('Shamasas, fleeing'). با چند مرد is a prepositional phrase 'with a few men' (چند = 'several/a few'). Word order is standard: modifier + name, then accompaniment expressed by با + noun phrase.
segments.s092-g84: برفتند is the past tense, 3rd person plural ('they departed/went away') and agrees with the plural action of Shamasas and his men. ازان = 'from that'. تیره گرد نبرد is a compact poetic noun phrase: تیره 'dark' modifies گرد used here as a noun/measure 'ring/round/circle', and نبرد ('battle') specifies its type — together 'the dark ring/circle of battle' (i.e. the battlefield or the ominous circle of fighting) from which they withdrew.
segments.s092-g17: کمانی is a noun meaning ‘(a) bow’ (here indefinite). افگند is the archaic/past form of انداختن 'to throw/place' (3rd person past). The phrase کمانی به بازو در افگند سخت literally orders: object (a bow) + prepositional phrase به بازو در (onto/into the arm) + verb (افگند) + adverb سخت ‘firmly/strongly’; سخت modifies the verb and appears after it as is common in older poetic Persian.
segments.s092-g18: یکی تیر is an emphatic subject phrase ‘one (single) arrow’. برسان here functions as a transitive verb meaning ‘bring/caused to reach’ (in classical poetry the final -د of برساند is sometimes dropped in meter). شاخ درخت is the goal/target ‘the branch of the tree’ (شاخ = branch, درخت = tree). Word order is subject (یکی تیر) → verb (برسان) → target (شاخ درخت).
segments.s092-g19: نگه کرد is a compound past: نگه (gaze/watch) + کرد (past of کردن) = ‘he looked/kept watch’. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here (‘to/so that’) and is followed by a wh-clause جای گردان کجاست ‘where the troop’s place/position is’. Note کجاست is کجا + ست (copula) in one word, the usual classical interrogative order.
segments.s092-g20: خدنگی is a noun (a dagger/short sword). The verb راند is past of راندن ‘to drive/drive in’ (3rd person past). The prepositional phrase به چرخ اندرون راند = ‘drove into the wheel’s inside’; اندرون is ‘inside/within’. راست at the end functions adverbially ‘straight/directly’. Word order is noun (instrument) → prepositional phrase marking goal/location → verb → adverb.
segments.s092-g21: بینداخت is past of انداختن with the verbal prefix بیـ (here 'he threw/overthrew'). The following noun phrase سه جای سه چوبه تیر uses numerals (سه) repeated for emphasis: ‘in three places, three staves/poles (related to the arrow)’. Classical Persian often places numeral + noun clusters after the verb; here they are the objects/affected items of بینداخت.
segments.s092-g22: برآمد is a past verb meaning ‘arose/issued forth’. It takes the following infinitival noun خروشیدن (‘to roar/roaring’) as its complement, so برآمد خروشیدن = ‘a roaring arose’. دار و گیر are the two nominative items causing that sound (joined by و). In this poetic line these nouns name the tree (دار) and a catching/peg element (گیر) that produced the outcry.
segments.s092-g23: چو شب روز شد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', then night + day + شد = 'when night became day'. انجمن شد سپاه is a terse, classical order: انجمن شد = 'an assembly occurred/was held' and سپاه (the army) is the agent/subject that assembled; the verb شد here functions as an intransitive past 'it happened/they gathered'.
segments.s092-g24: بران تیر کردند is past plural: بران (on that/thereat) + تیر کردند ‘they shot arrows’. هر کس نگاه follows to indicate that ‘each man (with his) gaze/aim’ — in older Persian the noun نگاه can play an adverbial role meaning ‘gazing/with aim’. Word order places the adverbial/agent phrase after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s092-g65: پس is an adverb meaning 'then' introducing what follows. اندر is a classical preposition meaning 'among/amongst' here, taking the noun phrase that follows. دلیران زاولستان is a genitive-style phrase 'the brave ones of Zabulistan' (دلیران = plural noun 'brave ones'; زاولستان is the place-name serving as the possessor/locator).
segments.s092-g66: برفتند is the 3rd-person-plural past form of رفتن with the prefix بر- (classical/poetic 'they went/advanced'). با introduces the accompaniment 'with', and شاه کابلستان is the noun phrase 'the king of Kabulistan' (شاه = king; کابلستان names his realm). The whole phrase says they moved along/with that king.
segments.s092-g67: چنان شد is an idiomatic phrase 'it became/it came to be so' (چنان = thus/so; شد = became/happened). ز is the poetic causal preposition 'because of/from', and بس is a quantifier here ('so much/so many' / 'a great number'), modifying کشته ('the slain'). در رزمگاه is the locative 'in/on the battlefield'.
segments.s092-g68: که introduces the result/clause 'so that/so one says'. گفتی here functions idiomatically as 'one would say' or 'it may be said' (a classical usage meaning 'so to speak' rather than the literal noun 'conversation'); جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world', تنگ شد is the predicate 'became narrow', and بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase marking the affected party ('for/upon the army').
segments.s092-g69: سوی introduces the direction/target 'toward' (here 'to the presence of'), followed by شاه ترکان 'the king of the Turks'. نهادند is the 3rd-person-plural past of نهادن 'they placed/laid', and سر is the direct object 'their head(s)' — together meaning they prostrated/laid their heads before the Turk king.
segments.s092-g70: گشاده سلیح and گسسته کمر are two descriptive phrases giving manner: گشاده 'open/drawn' modifies سلیح 'weapon(s)' (i.e. swords unsheathed), and گسسته 'undone/loosed' modifies کمر 'girdle/belt'. In classical Persian such adjective+noun groups describe the state in which they presented themselves.
segments.s092-g71: چون introduces a temporal clause 'when/as', so شماساس چون در بیابان رسید = 'when Shamasas reached the desert/wilderness'. در بیابان is the locative 'in the wilderness', and رسید is the 3rd-person-past 'he arrived/reached'.
segments.s092-g72: ز ره is the prepositional phrase 'from the road/way'. قارن کاوه are personal names presented in sequence (they are the ones who come), and آمد پدید is a poetic doublet of motion verbs — آمد 'came' plus پدید 'appeared' — meaning 'came forth/appeared'. The sequence therefore means 'from the road Qaran and Kaveh came into view.'
segments.s092-g73: که introduces a relative/clause ('who/that'); 'از لشکر ویسه' is a prepositional phrase 'from Viseh's army' (لشکر + name in postnominal position). برگشته بود is a compound past (برگشته = past participle 'returned', بود as auxiliary) yielding 'had returned' with verb-final placement typical of Persian.
segments.s092-g74: به خواری is an adverbial phrase of manner 'in humiliation' (به + noun). گرامیش را is the definite direct object marked by را ('his/her beloved/honoured one' as object). کشته بود is the past compound 'had killed' (کشته = past participle, بود auxiliary). Word order places the adverbial and object before the verb.
segments.s092-g75: به هم is a reciprocal adverbial ('towards one another'); بازخوردند is a 3rd-person plural past verb 'they moved/responded/turned (upon one another)'. هر دو سپاه is the subject 'both armies/hosts' (هر دو quantifier + noun). The verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion).
segments.s092-g76: A nominal phrase naming the participants: شماساس (proper name/title) followed by با + قارن marking accompaniment 'with Qaran'. کینه‌خواه is an epithet/adjective after the name meaning 'vengeful' or 'avenger', modifying قارن.
segments.s092-g77: بدانست is a past tense verb 'he knew/realized' (3rd singular). قارن is the subject. که introduces the subordinate clause. ایشان is the 3rd-person plural pronoun 'they'. کیند is an archaic/poetic present 3rd plural (equivalent to کنند) used here in reported speech 'they are doing / they intend'.
segments.s092-g78: ز زاولستان is a prepositional phrase 'from Zaulistan' (ز = poetic 'from'). ساخته is a participial/past form 'having made/assembled' (i.e., mustered). بر + چیند is a verbal construction where بر indicates direction/against and چیند (archaic form related to چیدن) means 'bring/lay/put (against)' or 'pluck/attack' in context; together they mean 'to bring/set against'.
segments.s092-g79: بزد is a simple past verb 'he struck/blew/struck out' (3rd singular). نای رویین is the object 'the iron reed/pipe' (نای = reed/instrument, رویین = iron/metallic). و connects clauses; بگرفت راه literally 'took the road' (بگرفت = 'seized/took up', used idiomatically here as 'set out', with راه = road/route).
segments.s092-g80: به پیش سپاه is a locative/goal phrase 'to/toward the front of the host' (به + پیش + سپاه). اندر آمد is a past verb phrase 'came in / entered' (اندر = poetic 'in/into', آمد = 'came'). The final سپاه repeats the noun as the subject/emphatic noun 'the host' following the verb, a common poetic inversion.

sentences.s092-s13: They said that this arrow was Zal's, and that was enough; no one had ever driven such an arrow into any bow.
sentences.s092-s14: When the shining sun rose from on high, a tumultuous cry went up from the plain.
sentences.s092-s15: Into the inner city the war-drum and trumpets sounded; bells and Indian (instruments) raised a clamor.
sentences.s092-s16: The army moved out onto the plain; they drew out the inner tent and brought forth the elephants.
sentences.s092-s17: He drew up his host before the (enemy) host when the Hamun plain appeared from around the black mountain.
sentences.s092-s18: The Khazruvans (rulers) of Daman, with spear and shield; one charged at Zal the Golden.
sentences.s092-s19: He struck with an upright spear upon his shining (breast), and that renowned cuirass of his split apart.
sentences.s092-s20: When the Zabulistan king shone forth, the Kabulistan contingents advanced.
sentences.s092-s21: Zal the brave put on armor and entered the battle like a lion.
sentences.s092-s22: He held his father's mace in his inner hand; his head had become full of rage and his heart full of blood.
sentences.s092-s23: He struck his head with the tawny mace; the ground was stained by his blood like a leopard's back.
sentences.s092-s24: He cast (it) off and handed (it) over, and from him he passed away; he came into the plain in front of the host.
sentences.s092-s5: Then he turned his face toward the city; when he entered the inner city, Namjuy.
sentences.s092-s6: He said to Mehrab, 'O alert man, approved in all his deeds.'
sentences.s092-s7: Now I will become (go) into the dark night; I will lay hands on someone and (bring) blood against them.
sentences.s092-s8: Let them know of me that I have returned; I came with a heart full and made for hatred.
sentences.s092-s29: He struck at Kalbad's belt, and thereupon struck the steel link of the chain on that band.
sentences.s092-s30: He sewed (a pad) into its middle and burned the army's heart toward Kalbad.
sentences.s092-s31: When these two were ashamed in battle, Shamasas became distraught and pale-faced.
sentences.s092-s32: Shamasas and that war-ready host were scattered from the battle and turned back.
sentences.s092-s1: The messenger drew near at hand; the fire of action seized his heart.
sentences.s092-s2: He turned his face toward the mihrab and forthwith rushed (on) with a warlike host.
sentences.s092-s3: When he saw Mehrab's foot planted in place, he perceived (that) knowledge and counsel were within his head.
sentences.s092-s4: He said to his heart: 'Now what fear is there of the host? What are kings before me — what but a handful of dust?'
sentences.s092-s25: He called for Shamasas and demanded the plot be brought out; when he would not come out, he drew (his weapon) and took pleasure in blood.
sentences.s092-s26: He found Kalbad within the inner quarters and put a steel (collar/chain) about his neck.
sentences.s092-s27: When the swordsman saw the mace in his hands, he at once made himself invisible to him.
sentences.s092-s28: Zal the rider strung the bow, and drove the dagger inward at him, bringing him low.
sentences.s092-s41: From that weary, beset host a man turned his face toward the shining sun.
sentences.s092-s42: Fleeing, Shamasas with a few men departed from that dark ring of battle.
sentences.s092-s9: He cast a bow firmly onto his arm; a single arrow struck the branch of a tree.
sentences.s092-s10: He watched to see where the troop’s position was; he drove a dagger straight into the inner part of the wheel.
sentences.s092-s11: He overthrew/struck down in three places three staves; the arrow sprang up and the tree and the peg cried aloud.
sentences.s092-s12: When night became day the army assembled; at that they shot arrows — each man (with) his gaze/aim.
sentences.s092-s33: Then they joined the brave men of Zabulistan, going with the king of Kabulistan.
sentences.s092-s34: So it happened — because of so many slain on the battlefield — that one might say the world had become narrow for the army.
sentences.s092-s35: They put their heads before the Turkish king, with swords drawn and belts undone.
sentences.s092-s36: When Shamasas reached the wilderness, Qaran and Kaveh appeared from the road.
sentences.s092-s37: who had returned from Viseh's army and had killed his beloved in humiliation.
sentences.s092-s38: Both hosts turned upon one another; Shamasas with Qaran the avenger.
sentences.s092-s39: Qaran realized that they were raising forces from Zaulistan to bring against (them).
sentences.s092-s40: He struck the iron reed (made the signal) and took the road; the host advanced into view.
